request.question: Name one of Bob Dylan's most-performed live songs, that is said to be inspired by the Prospect Park Water Tower in Minneapolis.

title: Kenwood Park Water Tower
Park Slope
Butler Square
Harry W. Jones House
Washburn Park Water Tower
All Along the Watchtower
Highland Park Tower
Prospect Park, Minneapolis
Prospect Park Water Tower
Old Chicago Water Tower District
sentences: The Kenwood Park Water Tower is an octagonal brick and stone water tower in the Kenwood neighborhood of Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States.
It was built in 1910 and designed by Frederick William Cappelen, the city of Minneapolis engineer at the time.
The tower is 110 feet tall, making it the tallest structure in Kenwood.
The tower has not been used to store water since 1954.
The tower was built to alleviate water pressure and storage problems in the Lowry Hill area.
Although the tower is not the work of a master architect or representative of a specific architectural style, its design is distinctive.
It is ornamented with projecting ribs, narrow rectangular windows, and Lombard bands, suggesting a medieval fortress.
The tower serves as a distinct visual focus within the neighborhood.
Park Slope is a neighborhood in northwest Brooklyn, New York City.
Park Slope is roughly bounded by Prospect Park and Prospect Park West to the east, Fourth Avenue to the west, Flatbush Avenue to the north, and Prospect Expressway to the south.

Generally, the section from Flatbush Avenue to Garfield Place (the "named streets") is considered the "North Slope", the section from 1st through 9th Streets is considered the "Center Slope", and south of 10th Street, the "South Slope".
The neighborhood takes its name from its location on the western slope of neighboring Prospect Park.
Fifth Avenue and Seventh Avenue are its primary commercial streets, while its east-west side streets are lined with brownstones and apartment buildings.
Butler Square (originally the Butler Brothers Company building) is a former warehouse and office building in Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States.
The building is located within the Minneapolis warehouse district and was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1971.
It is significant for its restrained Chicago School design by major Minneapolis architect Harry Wild Jones, and as a leading example of the older warehouse/office buildings in Minneapolis–Saint Paul.
Jones designed other buildings in Minneapolis such as the Minneapolis Scottish Rite Temple, Calvary Baptist Church, the Lakewood Cemetery Chapel, and the Washburn Park Water Tower.
The Harry W. Jones House is a historic house in Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States, in the Tangletown neighborhood.
Harry Wild Jones was one of the most notable architects in Minneapolis, with a career spanning nearly 50 years.
His designs included the Butler Brothers Warehouse, the chapel in Lakewood Cemetery, and the Washburn Park Water Tower.
His own home, built in 1887, was one of the first homes in the Washburn Park area and influenced the design of other homes in the area.
The house is listed on the National Register of Historic Places.

The Washburn Park Water Tower poses as a landmark of early 20th-Century architectural achievement within the Tangletown neighborhood in south Minneapolis, Minnesota, and has been doing so for nearly 75 years.

Perched on top of one of the highest points in south Minneapolis, the tower is given the privilege to boast its unique location and role as an unofficial "beacon" for incoming planes landing at Minneapolis-Saint Paul International Airport, yet remains hidden from much of the residents and visitors that pass by the base of the hill each day.

This is mainly because large homes and tall oak trees scatter the hillside where the tower resides, and even more so, because of the clustered mess of streets and dead ends that compromise the towers' occupancy.
Hence the name, 'Tangletown'.
"All Along the Watchtower" is a song written and recorded by American singer-songwriter Bob Dylan.
The song initially appeared on his 1967 album "John Wesley Harding", and it has been included on most of Dylan's subsequent greatest hits compilations.
Since the late 1970s, he has performed it in concert more than any of his other songs.
Different versions appear on four of Dylan's live albums.
The Highland Park Water Tower is a water tower in the Highland Park area of Saint Paul, Minnesota.
It was designed by Clarence W. Wigington, the nation's first African-American municipal architect.
The tower was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1986.
It was completed in 1928 at a cost of $69,483.
Prospect Park is a historic neighborhood within the University community of the U.S. city of Minneapolis, Minnesota.

The area is bounded by the Mississippi River to the south, the City of Saint Paul, Minnesota to the east, the Burlington Northern railroad yard to the north, and the Stadium Village commercial district of the University of Minnesota to the west.
The neighborhood is composed of several districts which include the East River Road area.
The 1913 Prospect Park Water Tower is a landmark and neighborhood icon.

The Prospect Park Water Tower, sometimes referred to as the "Witch's Hat Water Tower" or just "The Tower", is a historic water tower in the Prospect Park neighborhood of Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States.
It was built in 1913 on Tower Hill Park, a hilltop park established in 1906.
The water tower has become the neighborhood's architectural mascot for its singular design by Frederick William Cappelen.
The tower is rumored to be the inspiration for Bob Dylan's song "All Along the Watchtower," as the tower was clearly visible from Dylan's home in nearby Dinkytown.
The Old Chicago Water Tower District is a historic district along the Magnificent Mile shopping district in the Near North Side community area of Chicago, Illinois.
The district is located on both sides of North Michigan Avenue between East Chicago and East Pearson Streets.
It includes the Chicago Water Tower, Chicago Avenue Pumping Station, and Chicago Fire Department Fire Station No. 98.
All three structures are part of the Chicago Landmark district designated on October 6, 1971 (amended June 10, 1981).
The Water Tower and Pumping Station were jointly added to the National Register of Historic Places on April 23, 1975.
In addition the Tower was named an American Water Landmark in 1969.
The Water Tower was also one of the few buildings to survive the Great Chicago Fire.
The district is the namesake of the nearby Water Tower Place.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Name one of Bob Dylan's most-performed live songs, that is said to be inspired by the Prospect Park Water Tower in Minneapolis.
hop1_docs: Prospect Park Water Tower | The Prospect Park Water Tower, sometimes referred to as the "Witch's Hat Water Tower" or just "The Tower", is a historic water tower in the Prospect Park neighborhood of Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States.  It was built in 1913 on Tower Hill Park, a hilltop park established in 1906.  The water tower has become the neighborhood's architectural mascot for its singular design by Frederick William Cappelen.  The tower is rumored to be the inspiration for Bob Dylan's song "All Along the Watchtower," as the tower was clearly visible from Dylan's home in nearby Dinkytown.

As I Went Out One Morning | "As I Went Out One Morning" is a song written by Bob Dylan, released on his 1967 album "John Wesley Harding".  He has only performed this song live once, in the early phase of the Bob Dylan and The Band 1974 Tour.

Prospect Park, Minneapolis | Prospect Park is a historic neighborhood within the University community of the U.S. city of Minneapolis, Minnesota.  The area is bounded by the Mississippi River to the south, the City of Saint Paul, Minnesota to the east, the Burlington Northern railroad yard to the north, and the Stadium Village commercial district of the University of Minnesota to the west.  The neighborhood is composed of several districts which include the East River Road area.  The 1913 Prospect Park Water Tower is a landmark and neighborhood icon.

Shelter from the Storm | "Shelter from the Storm" is a song by Bob Dylan, released on his 15th studio album, "Blood on the Tracks", in 1975.  Along with "Tangled Up in Blue", "Shelter from the Storm" was one of two songs from "Blood on the Tracks" to be re-released on the 2000 compilation "The Essential Bob Dylan".  The song also appears on two live albums by Bob Dylan — "Hard Rain" (from a May 1976 performance) and "At Budokan" (recorded in February 1978).  A first take of the song, from the same recording session that produced the album track, is included on "The Best of Bob Dylan, Vol.  1" (1997).

All I Really Want to Do | "All I Really Want to Do" is a song written by Bob Dylan and featured on his Tom Wilson-produced 1964 album, "Another Side of Bob Dylan" ("see" 1964 in music).  It is arguably one of the most popular songs that Dylan wrote in the period immediately after he abandoned topical songwriting.  Within a year of its release on "Another Side of Bob Dylan", it had also become one of Dylan's most familiar songs to pop and rock audiences, due to hit cover versions by Cher and the Byrds.

The 30th Anniversary Concert Celebration | The 30th Anniversary Concert Celebration is a live double-album release in recognition of Bob Dylan's 30 years as a recording artist.  Recorded on October 16, 1992, at Madison Square Garden in New York City, it captures most of the concert, which featured many artists performing classic Dylan songs, before ending with three songs from Dylan himself.

All Along the Watchtower | "All Along the Watchtower" is a song written and recorded by American singer-songwriter Bob Dylan.  The song initially appeared on his 1967 album "John Wesley Harding", and it has been included on most of Dylan's subsequent greatest hits compilations.  Since the late 1970s, he has performed it in concert more than any of his other songs.  Different versions appear on four of Dylan's live albums.
hop1_titles: As I Went Out One Morning
All I Really Want to Do
response.summary_1: Passages say the Prospect Park Water Tower "is rumored to be the inspiration for Bob Dylan's song 'All Along the Watchtower, as the tower was clearly visible from Dylan's home in nearby Dinkytown.'" They also state that "'All Along the Watchtower' ... Since the late 1970s, he has performed it in concert more than any of his other songs." Bridge entity: the song title "All Along the Watchtower" — next hop (if needed) should retrieve sources confirming or disputing the rumor that the tower inspired that specific song (e.g., Dylan interviews, biographies, or local histories).
response.hop2_query: Prospect Park Water Tower "All Along the Watchtower" inspired Dylan rumor interview biography Minneapolis Dinkytown
hop2_docs: Prospect Park Water Tower | The Prospect Park Water Tower, sometimes referred to as the "Witch's Hat Water Tower" or just "The Tower", is a historic water tower in the Prospect Park neighborhood of Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States.  It was built in 1913 on Tower Hill Park, a hilltop park established in 1906.  The water tower has become the neighborhood's architectural mascot for its singular design by Frederick William Cappelen.  The tower is rumored to be the inspiration for Bob Dylan's song "All Along the Watchtower," as the tower was clearly visible from Dylan's home in nearby Dinkytown.

Prospect Park, Minneapolis | Prospect Park is a historic neighborhood within the University community of the U.S. city of Minneapolis, Minnesota.  The area is bounded by the Mississippi River to the south, the City of Saint Paul, Minnesota to the east, the Burlington Northern railroad yard to the north, and the Stadium Village commercial district of the University of Minnesota to the west.  The neighborhood is composed of several districts which include the East River Road area.  The 1913 Prospect Park Water Tower is a landmark and neighborhood icon.

All Along the Watchtower | "All Along the Watchtower" is a song written and recorded by American singer-songwriter Bob Dylan.  The song initially appeared on his 1967 album "John Wesley Harding", and it has been included on most of Dylan's subsequent greatest hits compilations.  Since the late 1970s, he has performed it in concert more than any of his other songs.  Different versions appear on four of Dylan's live albums.

Al's Breakfast | Al's Breakfast is reportedly the narrowest restaurant in the city of Minneapolis, at a width of 10 ft .  Al's Breakfast (Dinkytown Branch) is crammed into a former alleyway between two much larger buildings and is located in the city's Dinkytown neighborhood near the University of Minnesota.  The restaurant's 14 stools have seated generations of local students, along with notable figures such as writer James Lileks and humorist Garrison Keillor, all of whom consider the tiny diner to be a significant icon of the state.

Kenwood Park Water Tower | The Kenwood Park Water Tower is an octagonal brick and stone water tower in the Kenwood neighborhood of Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States.  It was built in 1910 and designed by Frederick William Cappelen, the city of Minneapolis engineer at the time.  The tower is 110 feet tall, making it the tallest structure in Kenwood.  The tower has not been used to store water since 1954.  The tower was built to alleviate water pressure and storage problems in the Lowry Hill area.  Although the tower is not the work of a master architect or representative of a specific architectural style, its design is distinctive.  It is ornamented with projecting ribs, narrow rectangular windows, and Lombard bands, suggesting a medieval fortress.  The tower serves as a distinct visual focus within the neighborhood.

Tower Hill Park | Tower Hill Park marks the northern boundary of Prospect Park, a hilly residential neighborhood in southeast Minneapolis.  The 4.7-acre city park is bounded on the northeast by University Avenue, the east by Clarence Avenue, the south by Seymour Avenue, the southwest by Orlin Avenue, and the northwest by Malcolm Avenue.  With the exception of University Avenue, quiet, residential streets surround Tower Hill Park.

Dinkytown | Dinkytown is a commercial district within the Marcy-Holmes residential neighborhood in Minneapolis, Minnesota.  Centered at 14th Avenue Southeast and 4th Street Southeast, the district contains several city blocks occupied by various small businesses, restaurants, bars, and apartment buildings that house mostly University of Minnesota students.  Though known for being the home of local businesses, the character has changed in recent years with a slight increase in more nationally-recognized chains, though many small local businesses still thrive.  Dinkytown is along the north side of the University of Minnesota Twin Cities East Bank campus.
response.summary_2: "All Along the Watchtower" is one of Bob Dylan's most-performed live songs (he has performed it in concert more than any of his other songs since the late 1970s). The Prospect Park Water Tower is rumored to have inspired that song, since the tower was clearly visible from Dylan's Dinkytown home (passage 1).
